sentences.text: I had my finger, you know, bandaged and things like that.
I had my arm in a sling, a little bit like that.
And we were marching from work back to the barracks.
The march, I don't know, must have been maybe a mile, a mile and a half.
And while we were marching back to the barrack, they were unloading a freight car with bread onto a truck.

As we were marching, I reached over with my right hand-- because this was my left hand-- and I had to force it, and I stole a loaf of bread from that truck, off of the-- yeah, from the truck on the car.
And I put it in my sling.

And when I got home-- we got to the barracks that night, I finished a whole loaf of bread, my portion of bread, my soup portion, and the two extra portions that I used to get from the-- from, from the guys, sort of things like that.
So, I mean, I was just wondering, how can-- you know, I must have weighed about all of 100 pounds, or something like that.
But I cleaned everything up.
I didn't leave a--
[LAUGHS] Didn't leave a drop.
Yeah.
Well-- Or this sub camp.
Yeah, the same diet, you know?
I, I-- they had some coffee or tea, but I don't know what.
It wasn't-- whatever it was, it wasn't much, you know?
But-- and at night, at night you used to have a soup and the bread.
In the morning, you used to have some kind of soup.
It was supposed to be like, like a, not a soup, maybe like cream of wheat or something.
But it wasn't the cream of wheat like my wife makes right now.
Yeah.
About seven, eight kilometers, something like that.
It wasn't far away.
No.
No, no.
No.
No.
No, they didn't even ask your permission.
No.
No.
No.
No.
What else can you tell me about the conditions, the living conditions, of what you observed?
Well, the-- you know, in that-- in that camp, you know, especially there was-- there were bullies.
You know, like in that particular camp, there was this one guy-- there was another guy, my same-- in my age.
And we were already-- you know, when like this other guy and myself-- we were like already Birkenau survivors.
[LAUGHS] Like, you, you weren't a greenhorn.
You were an old-timer.
And there was this one-- in this same camp, there was this one guy.
He was like a bully.
He was about six feet, two inches.
He was a prisoner.
He didn't have any authority.
He didn't anything like that.
But when-- just when we got there, right after we got there, the people were outside clearing the stumps and the woods and everything, and freezing to death.
And they didn't have any clothes, and their hands frost-bitten.
They used to want to run in because for some reason, they managed to have that stove heating, you know, with wood or something.
They had plenty of wood, because cleaning all the stumps with the thing.
So in-- and this other kid and myself, you know, we didn't work that much.
We stayed more in the camp, so thing like that.
So he-- if somebody used to come in, this guy would be a bully.
Chase them out.
And one time I said-- you know, this-- the other guy, [INAUDIBLE], said, we just attacked the guy and threw him out.
And I said, you do that one more time, you know-- we just kind of looked out for the underdog a little bit, you know?
And you just tried to survive, you know?
It, it-- the work over there, the people where we did the work, they-- while you were working, the, the Germans, you know, that were in charge and things like that, they were more humane.
They weren't like, like you-- like you see some-- you work, and you didn't work fast enough, they come with a whip or something like that.
It was a little more-- but I remember another thing, you know?
OK, now this was close to the war was ending.
When was that, then?
That was in, in April, March, April of, of '45.
So we were marching once to work, and they had already American planes throwing bombs in the area.
So I know we were marching to work, and it was an open field.
And all of a sudden, there comes the bombs.
And everybody's running, you know?
The Germans and everybody running in the bunkers and everything, like to hide.
And there was a pile of hay or something.
And I just got up on the top of it, and started waving.
I could've got shot for doing that.
But, I mean, that was the joy.
[LAUGHS]
I didn't care if a bomb hit you.
At least, you know?
[LAUGHS] Did you see some bombs fall?
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, no.
We were-- we were-- it was a little bit away from camp.
No, they didn't hit the camp.
No.
They didn't hit the camp.
It was--
Oh, I was-- I, I I wished I-- they would throw more bombs, you know, to just clear it out, something like that.
I was happy.
We were happy.
No.
I was never in it.
No.
I was never there.
But when it got closer, and then they-- then they took us a forced march.
Then we-- you know, the closer the Germans got, then we went-- then they marched us out from the camp.
And they were marching us to-- I don't know where to, you know?
But they were-- we were on a march because we were liberated on, on the road.
And when we were marching, that one time, we were marching and the planes came.
And they-- and they-- [CLEARS THROAT] Like skydived.
You know, dived down to see, because they didn't-- at that particular time, they didn't have an anti-aircraft, much of the anti-aircraft, because it was almost all knocked out.
So when they skydived down and they saw us-- so I could-- we could see.
Like, they would circle and come down below us, and down in the field, whatever else they were shooting at it.
And all of a sudden, you could see another fire coming up and things like that.
And we were just in heaven.
And at that time, did they have appels at this camp, as well?
Was the--
Oh yeah.
No, not any different than anything.
Get up in the morning, you know?
Get-- have some soup.
Get lined up and march to work.
Work a whole day, get back, it was dark already, had something to eat, and go to sleep.
Oh yeah.
Yeah.
Once, once we were in the barracks.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Oh yeah.
Because we were just hoping, you know?
It was getting a different situation.
It was getting close.
Whenever something like this come on, they were hiding.
They, they-- when we-- you know, like, lay down on the ground, they, they didn't want to be seen, SS.
They didn't want to see the ground.
They were just coming closer to the prisoners, [? laying ?] the other prisoner, you know, something like that.
Then when it's over, they start it again, you know?
But in our march, you know, it was just kind of-- gee.
I don't even remember, you know?
I mean, we were marching for a week, or something.
Did you have shoes?
Yeah.
Wooden shoes or something.
Well, that was already-- that was already in April.
It was warm.
And it wasn't in, in-- near-- Dachau was near Bavaria, so it wasn't as cold there then.
It was already warmer a little.
Yeah.
It was warm.
It wasn't-- I don't know exactly what the temperature was, but it was warmer.
No, some [INAUDIBLE].
I think I was wearing the, the, the striped clothes, you know?
You were wearing the same striped clothes, you know?
Marching?
It was about-- it, it must have been about close to a week or something like that, three or four days.
And then, and then when we got closer, and-- that was already close to the-- [CLEARS THROAT] --for the Germans losing the war because you could see the bombings more often, and things like that.
And then, I don't know what day it was, and then one high-ranking German officer-- not SS, but Wehrmacht-- drove by, and he saw him.

And-- [CLEARS THROAT] --I don't know who to, whichever SS man, he talked to, and he said that not to march us anymore, not to kill us, but to leave us and turn us over to the American army, to the American Army, or whoever is there.
[CLEARS THROAT] And they agreed to it.
And we were led into a, a place which was nearby.
I don't know.
It wasn't a camp or something.
[CLEARS THROAT] But-- and we stayed there for almost a day, the whole day.
And then in the evening, we heard that an SS group from Auschwitz, and one of them that we knew, a [GERMAN] from Auschwitz, a real-- the worst, the worst of the worst, was in the thing.
In the-- in that-- in that holding-- they were going to hold him and turn over.
Yeah.
And he says he doesn't take any orders from the Wehrmacht.
I don't care whether they're generals or nothing.
And he's going to take us with them to march us.
And I guess we were too many of us.
[CLEARS THROAT] And he took a smaller group, and he was supposed to come back for the rest of us.
And that's when I hid out in, in that-- in the hall, what it was.
They had an attic, and I-- I don't know exactly how it was.
I climbed up in the attic, and on a four by two or two by four, I laid all night, because I didn't know if they were going to come back for us or not.
And evidently, they didn't come back for us.
In the following day, but, like, nobody showed up.
And we-- I got off the attic, and it was just-- like you didn't see nobody.
You didn't see any SS.
You saw prisoners and things like that.
And then about an hour or two later-- in that morning, you heard something like a bridge was blowing.
And an hour or two later, we saw the first Jeep with American soldiers.
Yeah.
And that was a good sight.
Yeah.
That was May 1, 1945.
Yeah.
Well, that day, you know, myself, for some reason, I wasn't as weak as some of, you know, some of the rest of the prisoners.
For some reason, I had some strength.
And when we were liberated, we were just-- went wild, or something like that.
So I remember just going in-- some of the houses, they were empty, because even the German people, they were running away or something.
It could be maybe the SS lived around there, and they were running away.
So I remember going in a house, and it was empty, and to take a shower.
I think I remember I took a shower.
And I took-- and I found some clothes in there and changed clothes.
I remember that.
I don't remember whether I had any food or not, but I remember taking a shower and changing clothes.
And then, and then when-- and then we-- then we lived on a farm, you know?
Because some of the guys, the prisoners, they rode with some American-- the American military police with some of the army, you know, to try to find the Gestapo, or the SS, and things like that.
And I remember an incident that this one-- they found this one SS man, and the-- and the, the-- besides the American army.
The one guy that was with the American Army at that time, he was, he was also a Jewish prisoner, but I think he was going-- he was under a Polish name, an assumed Polish name.
And when they brought him in to, to the farm that we at, and he said to that Polish guy, he says, don't leave me here, because you know I didn't do nothing to you.
Or the only thing I did was to the Jews.
And they brought him in, and they, you know, they beat him up.
But I, I couldn't do it, I couldn't stand there.
I walked away, you know?
These incidents-- Do you recall where you were at that [INAUDIBLE]?
Yeah, I was near-- in a-- in a farm near Landsberg.
It was the Landsberg mine.
It was a DP camp.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
It, it was right outside Landsberg, you know?
And there was a big, big DP camp, displaced persons camp, in-- near Landsberg.
Yeah.
And we were about maybe 10 kilometers from there.
And when were you reunited with your family?
Well, OK, I went back-- when I went to Landsberg to the camp, I found somebody from my home town.
And he told me that my brother survived.
I said, I had two brothers.
He said, well, I don't know about two, but I know of one of them, which is your oldest, survived.
And that was in Poland someplace, near Poland.
And I said, do you know where he might have gone?
And he says, no, but I know he went back to Poland.
So then, from that time, I-- from that farm that I went-- lived, I took off for Munich, which was about 50, 60 kilometers.
In Munich, I don't know.
I don't even remember how I met these people.
In Munich.
I ended at this one place which was [NON-ENGLISH].
I never forget, [NON-ENGLISH].
And I met several people from my hometown.
And after a few days, I told them that I want to go to Poland.
So about four of us took off from Munich to Poland.
We didn't have any-- we had some money.
We didn't have any plane tickets, or train tickets, or nothing.
We just hitch-hiked rides by trucks, cars, whatever it was, trains.
So we finally wind up in Prague.
And we, we got to Prague on the railroad, by train.
When we got off from the train, as we were walking down the platform in Prague in the railroad, we were going one way.
Facing us, coming the other way, was somebody from our hometown that one of my sisters used to work for his parents.
They had the store.
And when we met him, he told us that my two sisters are alive, and they survived.
And they're in Poland, and they don't live in-- and he gave us the address where they are.
And they didn't live in our hometown.
They lived in Sosnowiec.
And him and every one of us, all four of us, found somebody.
One of them found a brother.
And I found my two sisters.
So-- but when we got there, that was in, in the-- at that time, after the war, Poland was more anti-Semitic than before the war.
We got to Katowice first.
And we wanted to take a train from Katowice-- I mean, a rail-- it was from Katowice to Sosnowiec.
It was very close-- a street car.
And we basically had to hide, you know?
You know, not to be recognized as Jews, even after the war in Poland.
And so, when we got that-- we had an address.
So we got there, and they didn't know where they-- he didn't know were the other people, there, sisters and brothers.
I said, let's go to my place-- I don't remember whether he give me addresses or not-- to where my sister is, and then we'll see if they can find.
So anyhow, to-- we got to my sister's, I ring the bell, or knock on the door, and somebody opens the door.
And it wasn't my sister, because they happened to have-- there was a wedding going on that evening.
A girlfriend of theirs got married, and they had the biggest apartment, a two-bedroom apartment, so the wedding was in their house.
So I said, whoever opened the door, I don't remember, and they say, yes, can I help you?
I said, well, who is-- is-- I'm looking for Sabina or Helen Price.
Do-- they said, well, what are you-- who are you?
What are-- I said, I'm their brother.
And she run in to get my sisters, you know?
And my older sister fainted.
And my youngest-- and my brother was there, too.
And everybody, the whole family, was there, in the thing like that.
So was it a nice reunion that time, you know?
And from there, where did you go?
From there, my sister said to me, you know, that when they survived-- when we gave a lot of belongings to the farmer that-- to that farmer, and to other farmers.
And when my sister got back, she, she went to the farm, asking maybe they can give us something back, or help or something.
They said, we don't know you.
If your brother, or something-- like, my brother.
I was the baby.
So my brother was-- they were doing some business in the farm.
They had some clothes they were selling, to do something to make-- to survive.
So my brother and another couple from-- that lived there already, they were liberated earlier, they were going to our hometown.
So I said, I'll go with you guys.
So I went with them to [?
Volbroch. ?] We got there around sunset-- I don't know what time-- and we were going to stay at this one farm overnight.
In the barn, not in a hotel, in the barn, staying there.
So he, around 10 o'clock at night, he comes in and he says, my daughter overheard something that somebody saw us arrive in town.
And he-- they might-- somebody might want to hurt us.
And he doesn't want us to get hurt in town, and everything like that.
That he rather we would leave.
And this was dark-- pitch-- you know, you don't have lighting like you have in here.
In a, in a farm in Poland--
Yeah, from then--
We left.
We left.
And we wanted to go back to Sosnowiec, and we stopped about halfway down the road.
There was a train, a train, in their station.
And we got there, and there was this Polish lieutenant.
And he said, Jews.
The only thing you're good is to take you to the woods and to kill you like dogs.
And I really thought he was going to do it.
But they all had money.
So he took their money away, and he let them go.
And me, he was-- he wrote up a big report.
He was going to keep me because I didn't honor the Polish government.
I didn't come to the Poland legally, because I didn't wait on the border for two days to get a permission to come in, because when we got off, we jumped the train and everything.
But since he took everything away from this other guy begged him, let him go, let him go.
So he let me go, and we went back to Sosnowiec.
And I told my sisters, I says-- and I really thought after surviving Auschwitz, Birkenau, Dachau, everything, that I'm going to get killed in Poland.
So I told my sisters, I said, I don't know about you guys, if you're going to stay here.
But I'm getting out of here, I'm getting out of here as fast as I can.
So they said, we want to leave, too.
And they took about a couple weeks.
Give us a couple weeks, we'll liquidate it.
And we all went back to Germany.
Munich.
We went--
In 1949 I came to--
Yeah.
It was in-- when I got to United States, was September.
It was Labor Day.
And I-- and I-- we landed in New Orleans, instead of New York because they told me-- because I came over on, through the [INAUDIBLE], you know, not by--
The relief--
I wasn't sponsored by anybody particular.
So I was going to come to Stockton.
So being I was coming to Stockton, so the ship landed in New Orleans.
So I remember very well that day because we were wearing that old [NON-ENGLISH] and the clothes.
And you could see these river boats with with these people going on them.
Who was with you?
I was by myself.
Yeah.
By myself.
From there, when we-- when we landed there, somebody came over and they said, instead of going to Stockton, we don't have any place for you in Stockton.
We're going to send you to Nashville, Tennessee.
But I didn't know any difference between Stockton or Nashville, I didn't know anything.
So I said, fine with me.
So we stayed in New Orleans for-- we-- OK.
We couldn't get off the boat because it was Labor Day.
We stayed there overnight.
And the next day, we got off the boat and we stayed in New Orleans till about towards 9, 10 o'clock at night.
And that's when I took a train and went to Nashville.
I think the train ride took about 18 or 24 hours, because we didn't get to Nashville till the following evening.
In Nashville, I was met by somebody from the-- Nashville had a large Jewish community center, and I was met by somebody.
And they were-- after-- they got me a room in a boarding house, you know?
It was a Jewish lady.
She had about two boarders.
I was the third one.
They give-- they asked me if I have any money.
I had a few dollars, maybe $200, something like that.
And they paid my room, paid my room.
And she introduced me to-- it took me-- from the boarding house, there was a restaurant not too far.
And she took me to the restaurant, ordered me some, I don't know, some food.
And I went to this restaurant for about two weeks until I found a job.
And I don't remember whether it was ham and eggs, or bacon, and I ate this twice a day.
[LAUGHS] I remember, like now, this one waitress, she was trying to help me to order something else and everything, but it didn't come out.
So I ordered that.
And after two weeks alone, after about two weeks, I found a job as a watch maker, because I learned watch making before I came over to the States at the Jewish-- a Jewish store.
And I started working, and then I started going to lunch with some of the salesman, you know, and we eat in cafeterias.
And I-- even for a certain length of time, I used to point.
I have this or this.
And naturally, I wasn't used to the food, but you ate something.
And then I started-- I went to school two nights a week, public night school.
Two nights a week I went to Jewish Community Center.
On Sundays, this-- another Jewish lady had a class to teach us English.
And the one night that I didn't-- I used go to movies, like the Western for a quarter.
So two movies.
I met-- well, then-- not till-- that was 1949.
I didn't meet my wife till 1961.
In, in the state.
In, in-- here, in Culver City.
In California.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Her name is Shirley.
It was Shirley Lavine, now it's Shirley Price.
Yeah.
I have three children.
Michael is my oldest.
He's 33, Debbie, the middle one, she's 31, and my youngest one is David.
He's 26.
